description: Wardley evolution property reference: 12 properties with 4-phase descriptions for solution evaluation. Based on Simon Wardley's evolution characteristics model.

phases.1: Undefined market; no established demand or supply dynamics exist yet
phases.2: Emerging market with early adopters exploring custom solutions and bespoke offerings
phases.3: Established market with growing competition, clear demand, and multiple recognized vendors
phases.4: Mature, commoditized market with stable demand, high volume, and price-driven competition

phases.1: Knowledge is scarce, tacit, and held by few experts or inventors; very little documented
phases.2: Knowledge is growing but fragmented across early practitioners; shared via informal communities
phases.3: Knowledge is widely published and taught; best practices, certifications, and formal training emerge
phases.4: Knowledge is ubiquitous, embedded in operations; considered baseline competency, extensively automated

phases.1: Poorly understood or unknown to the broader market; seen as experimental or unproven
phases.2: Recognized as a niche solution by early adopters; increasing awareness but limited mainstream trust
phases.3: Well-understood and accepted across the market; perceived as a proven, reliable solution category
phases.4: Taken for granted; invisible infrastructure that the market expects as a standard utility

phases.1: Users see it as novel and experimental; willingness to tolerate imperfections for innovation
phases.2: Users perceive differentiation value; willing to invest in learning and customization
phases.3: Users expect feature completeness, reliability, and support; they compare alternatives systematically
phases.4: Users expect it to just work; seen as a commodity with minimal differentiation or switching cost

phases.1: Industry views it as a research curiosity or science project; not yet taken seriously for production
phases.2: Industry acknowledges potential; early competitive advantages emerge for adopters
phases.3: Industry recognizes it as a strategic necessity; analysts track it, standards bodies engage
phases.4: Industry treats it as essential infrastructure; failure to adopt is a competitive disadvantage

phases.1: Value derived from novelty, exploration, and future potential; high risk tolerance
phases.2: Value derived from differentiation and competitive advantage through tailored solutions
phases.3: Value derived from reliability, feature richness, total cost of ownership, and ecosystem integration
phases.4: Value derived from cost efficiency, operational excellence, standardization, and scale economics

phases.1: Very poorly understood; the problem space is still being defined and explored
phases.2: Increasingly understood by specialists; solution patterns emerge but vary across implementations
phases.3: Well-understood with established architectures, reference models, and documented trade-offs
phases.4: Completely understood; commoditized knowledge embedded in standard operating procedures and automation

phases.1: No meaningful comparison possible; each implementation is unique and bespoke
phases.2: Comparison is possible but difficult; solutions differ significantly in approach and scope
phases.3: Feature-by-feature comparison is standard; industry benchmarks, analyst reports, and reviews available
phases.4: Comparison is trivial; solutions are interchangeable with focus on price, SLA, and availability

phases.1: High failure rates expected and tolerated; experimentation inherently involves failure
phases.2: Failures are common but decreasing; deficiencies are specific and addressed through iteration
phases.3: Failures are notable events; deficiencies are tracked via SLAs, bug databases, and quality metrics
phases.4: Failure is unacceptable and highly visible; deficiencies are rare and demand immediate remediation

phases.1: Exploration and experimentation; building prototypes and proofs of concept with no established channels
phases.2: Early sales through direct engagement; custom contracts, consulting, and bespoke delivery
phases.3: Product marketing and competitive positioning; standardized sales channels, partnerships, and ecosystems
phases.4: Volume-based procurement; API-driven consumption, self-service portals, and utility pricing models

phases.1: Very low efficiency; high resource investment per unit of output, significant waste in exploration
phases.2: Improving efficiency through learning; reducing waste as patterns and best practices emerge
phases.3: Good efficiency with established processes; measurable ROI, optimized delivery, and scaling operations
phases.4: Maximum efficiency through standardization, automation, and economies of scale; marginal cost approaches zero

phases.1: Decisions driven by vision, intuition, and strategic bets on uncertain future value
phases.2: Decisions driven by competitive differentiation, first-mover advantage, and strategic fit
phases.3: Decisions driven by feature comparison, total cost of ownership, vendor stability, and risk mitigation
phases.4: Decisions driven by price, availability, compliance, and operational convenience

metadata.notes: Weights are approximately 1/12 (0.0833...). Actual computation should use 1/N where N is the number of evaluated properties to handle partial evaluation gracefully.
